title: Population Policy and Reproduction in Singapore: Making Future Citizens By Shirley Hsiao-Li Sun Routledge. 2011. 208 pages. $145 cloth
abstract: Singapore, a small city-state that can only boast of her people as resource, has been suffering sustained decline in fertility since the 1980s. Singlehood statistics have also inched up, and in a fairly tradition society where procreation is sanctioned only within a legally recognized union, there is little hope that the total fertility rate (TFR) will go up any time soon. To augment the shortfall in labor supply, the government had resorted to expansion of the nonresident population. However, recent antiforeigner sentiments have sent loud signals to the leadership that this is not acceptable to a significant segment of the local population. Sun's book is focused on an extremely important issue of strong social significance, and one in which there is an urgent need for academic discourse that can debunk the myth that surrounds marriage and procreation decisions. She starts off very promising, with a comprehensive discussion on the notion of citizenship and its implications on state policies in Chapter 1. I expected that the following chapters would advance the interesting interplay between state initiatives, perception of citizenship rights and responsibilities and perhaps address the contentious issue of childbearing as a duty of responsible citizens. But, unfortunately, the progression that follows tends to be more descriptive and lacks explanatory power.
